message:
perf+correctness(lexical): iterative edge-ngram generation; cap at 20 chars; add test coverage

`cass_generate_edge_ngrams` previously allocated a `Vec<usize>` of
up to `MAX_NGRAM_INDICES = 21` character boundaries per word, then
iterated it to emit prefixes. For long words (e.g. long file paths,
camelCase identifiers > 20 chars, compound tokens) this allocated
transient heap memory on every call, and the 21-index cap let the
loop emit a 21-char prefix on top of the intended 20.

Rewrite the inner loop to avoid the `Vec` entirely:

- Walk `word.char_indices()` once, tracking `char_count` by hand.
- Skip the first character (we emit prefixes of length ≥ 2).
- Stop emitting after `char_count > MAX_NGRAM_CHARS = 20` so the
  cap now bounds the output at exactly 20 characters of inclusive
  prefix.
- Compute `end_idx` at each step as `start_idx + ch.len_utf8()` so
  multi-byte characters (the éclair test below) still slice at a
  valid UTF-8 boundary.

Result: same output for the ≤ 20-char common case, O(1) extra
allocation per word, and a strict 20-char cap rather than the
previous 21.

Tests:
- `cass_generate_edge_ngrams_emits_expected_prefixes` locks in the
  baseline ASCII behavior (`"hello world"` produces
  `"he hel hell hello wo wor worl world"`), asserts multi-byte
  support (`"éclair"` produces `"éc écl écla éclai éclair"`), and
  asserts the single-char input returns an empty string.
- `cass_generate_edge_ngrams_caps_prefixes_at_twenty_chars` asserts
  the 20-char cap on a 25-char input — the output stops at the
  `"abcdefghijklmnopqrst"` prefix, which the previous 21-index code
  would have allowed to run one extra character longer.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
